title: A Strange Space Explosion Seen by a Big Telescope
summary: Scientists using the James Webb Space Telescope saw a mysterious explosion in space that lasted much longer than usual. This explosion, called GRB 250702B, is a type of event that happens when a big star collapses into a black hole, but this one lasted for hours instead of seconds. Astronomers like Huei Sears from Rutgers are studying it because it behaves differently, with X-ray activity seen a day before. Many telescopes around the world, including ones from NASA and China, helped watch it. They think it might involve a black hole merging with a star or something else unusual. The explosion happened in a galaxy far away, about 8 billion light-years from Earth, which means it occurred long ago. Scientists are still trying to figure out what caused this strange event.

a place in space with very strong gravity that pulls things in
a huge group of stars, like our Milky Way
people who study things to learn how they work

questions.correct_answer: A black hole merging with a star

questions.question: How far away is the galaxy where the explosion happened?
Why are scientists confused about this explosion?

background_read: Space is full of amazing events like explosions called gamma-ray bursts, which happen when big stars collapse into black holes. Usually, these bursts are very short, but scientists using telescopes like the James Webb Space Telescope found one that lasted much longer, which is unusual. They use tools from around the world to study it because it might teach us new things about how black holes and stars work in the universe.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists like Huei Sears and teams from NASA and other countries
WHAT: A strange space explosion that lasted hours instead of seconds
WHERE: In a galaxy about 8 billion light-years away from Earth
WHY: To learn why it behaved differently and what it tells us about space

perspectives.description: Scientists are excited to study this event because it might reveal new physics about explosions in space.
